text:
Comparing Quantities Unit Overview

Click here to view the Comparing Quantities Unit Planning Chart (including pacing, key, terms, and resources)

Unit Description

This unit is devoted to an introduction to ratios and reasoning with ratios, also called proportional reasoning. A ratio is a comparison of two quantities that depends on multiplication and division. A difference, by contrast, is a comparison of two quantities that depends on addition and subtraction. The latter does not represent a ratio. Mathematically, students typically come to grade 6 thinking additively, and they tend to compare using differences. In this unit, they will learn that comparing with ratios is better suited for certain situations. This work builds upon the concepts in Number Connections and equivalent fractions. Equivalence of ratios is introduced in Investigation 1 and continues throughout the unit. The students use rate tables as a way to express equivalent ratios and compute unit rates. In grade 7, equivalent ratios are called proportional relationships.

Working with equivalent ratios is similar to working with equivalent fractions. For most of this unit, however, ratios are not written as fractions. The intent is to keep the notation for part-whole fractions and rational numbers apart from the notation for ratio comparisons. It will help students to keep these ideas separate as they develop understanding of ratio. When the word fraction appears, it is used to represent part of a whole. Equivalent ratios can be represented in tables, tape diagrams, double number lines, grids, and bars. Percent is introduced in Investigation 4 as a ratio. The main goal of this unit is to begin the process of recognizing a ratio situation and then applying an appropriate strategy for solving the problem.

The development of algebraic reasoning continues in this unit as an opportunity to generalize patterns that occur in ratio tables. This continues the theme that algebraic expressions and equations represent important number properties and relationships among quantities.

Reasoning with ratios is revisited and deepened throughout grade 7 as a context to study more complex numeric situations, similarity (scale drawings), linearity, probability, and data. In grade 7, reasoning with ratios becomes proportional reasoning, which involves proportional relationships.

As with all of the Connected Mathematics® 4 units, one Mathematical Reflection guides the development of the understanding of the mathematical ideas in the unit.

Mathematical Reflection

In this unit, we are exploring problems that involve reasoning with ratios. At the end of this investigation, ask yourself:

What do you understand about ratios and reasoning with ratio?

Summary of Investigations

Investigation 1: Making Comparisons

Students explore ratios in the mathematical context of understanding and making comparison statements. Understanding ratios and equivalence of ratios is a major emphasis. In this unit, ratios are comparisons between numbers. They use visual models, symbols, and language to express ratio comparisons. The measuring of progress in a school fundraiser focuses students' attention on ratios as they compare the goals and progress of different grades. A comparison statement that represents a multiplicative relationship between the two quantities is a ratio. These statements can be represented by "for every" statements. For example, for every $3 the Washington students plan to raise, the Central students plan to raise $1. Problem 1.1 uses comparison statements about fundraising goals of three schools to introduce multiple ways to describe a situation. In Problem 1.2, students focus on ratios as comparisons using the context of students preferences for pizza. selling cupcakes as a fundraiser is the context for Problem 1.3 to develop part-to-part or part-to-whole ratios.

Investigation 2: Using Ratios to Solve Problems

Using what they know about comparison statements and equivalent fractions, students develop strategies for finding equivalent ratios. In Problem 2.1, the context of making and packaging cupcakes provides and opportunity to look at comparison statements that involve part-to-part comparisons and part-to-whole comparisons. For example, for every 8 green cupcakes, there are 10 white cupcakes is a apart-to -part comparison. For every 20 cupcakes, there are 8 green cupcakes is a part-to-whole comparison. In Problem 2.2, students create equivalent ratios by scaling up or down by multiplying each quantity by the same number (scale factor). Ratio tables are used to represent equivalent ratios. These tables allow students to look for general patterns and express these patterns in words and algebraic expressions. Sharing smoothie bars provides a visual model similar to a tape diagram or a bar to find and represent equivalent ratios in Problem 2.3. This allows students to learn about partitioning while keeping track of the relationships among shares of segments of a bar. Students write equivalent ratios to represent the partitioning.

Investigation 3: Using Unit Rates and Rate Tables

Unit Rates are introduced in Problem 3.1 as a specific kind of ratio in which one quantity being compared is 1 unit. rate tables are an extension of ratio tables and are generally used when the two quantities being compared have different units if measurement. For example, for every 6boxes of smoothie bars, it costs $48. The unit rate for 1 box is $8. this can be found by scaling the original ratio/rate down a unit rate. Rate table and visual models are important strategies for developing understanding of ratios and unit rates. Problem 3.2 has students encounter two-unit rtes for a situation using the context of the relationship between the number of ounces of popcorn kernels and number of cups of popcorn. Problem 3.3 uses a recipe to make slime to continue looking at scaling quantities up or down. The setting of a walking backward race in Problem 3.4 gives students the setting ti refine their strategies for using ratios.

Investigation 4: For Every 100: Introducing Percent

Percent is introduced as a comparison statement in which one of the quantities is 100. For every 100 students, there are 30 sixth graders. This can also be written as 30 out of 100 students are sixth graders or 30% of the students are sixth graders. In Problem 4.1, students look at survey data to make comparisons using percents. The percent bar, tape diagrams, and tables are used to visualize and analyze situations involving ratios. They are also useful for relating ratios, fractions, and percents. Problem 4.2 uses a percent bar or tape diagram to analyze survey data. Students use percents to make comparisons among the frequencies of various genetic traits in a class of sixth-grade students in Problem 4.3. In Problem 4.4, using knowledge about a percentage of a given total, students also make predictions using the strategies listed above. They also use these tools to fina the whole given the percent and a part.

Mathematics Overview:

Major Focus: proportional reasoning

Key ideas: ratio as a multiplicative comparison of quantities; part-to-part or part-to-whole; unit rate; equivalent ratios; percent as ratio; proportional reasoning from tables, tape diagrams

Strategic Connections: algebraic expressions, equations, graphs

Connected Mathematics® 4 develops proportional reasoning over the course of several units. Comparing Quantities is the first unit to explore this idea. But it builds off the Variables and Patterns and Number Connections units. For example. in Comparing Quantities, cupcakes are packaged and sold according to the ratio: for every 5 boxes of cupcakes sold, $60 is collected. How much money A is collected for N boxes? Equations and graphs of linear equations are revisited. The equations A = 12N represents the amount A collected for the number of boxes of cupcakes N sold at $12 per box. Students see the equation snd graphic representation from the Variables and Patterns unit as a proportional relationship. The number of turns N a Ferris Wheel makes in t seconds is revisited from the Number Connections unit.

All subsequent units in grade 6 take advantage of the groundwork done here to apply this concept in different contexts, deepening and strengthening student understanding. Thus, proportional reasoning is a unifying theme in all grade 6 and grade 7 units. For example, in the Covering and Surrounding unit, students investigate the ratio of circumference of a circle to its diameter.

Curriculum Note: We could call this reasoning about ratios. Reasoning about ratios is the same as reasoning bout proportional relationships; the underlying idea is the same. A ratio is a comparison of two quantities, A:B ; a proportion compares two ratios, A:B = C:D. Students develop strategies to solve proportions in grade 7, but they are not new ideas. Students use these ideas in Comparing Quantities. Scaling a ratio or a rate or a measurement is the same as solving a proportion.

Link to the Future: In grade 7, students explore proportional reasoning in the context of scale drawings in Stretching and Shrinking and, in multiple contexts, in Comparing and Scaling. Students develop strategies for solving ant proportion, for example, 3:2x = 7:5. In Moving Straight Ahead, students compare linear equations that represent proportions, like y = ax, and those that do not, like y = ax + b. How Likely Is It? and Samples and Populations use proportional reasoning to develop understanding of probability and statistics.

Looking Back: Students made tables and graphs of proportional relationships, like d = 50t, in Variables and Patterns. At that point, they did not have the vocabulary about ratios to describe the patterns they saw, but they may have used language like "For every 1 hour, the distance covered is 50 miles."

In roughly this order, in Comparing Quantities, students so the following.

  • Understand that a ratio is a multiplicative way of comparing quantities.
    • Compare part-to-part and part-to-whole ratios.
    • Understand that a unit rate is. particular ratio where one of the quantities is 1.
  • Use proportional reasoning strategies to solve problems involving ratios.
    • Connect table and formula representations of equivalent ratios to graphic representations of proportional representations.
    • Use tools such as tables and tape diagrams and double number lines to solve problems with unit rates and percents.

Ratios as Multiplicative Comparisons

This unit builds multiplicative thinking about comparisons. Students may initially have trouble moving away from comparisons expressed as differences. Suppose there are 150 sixth graders and 100 seventh graders in a school. Students may initially only see 150 sixth graders as 50 more than 100 seventh graders (additive thinking) snd not as 150 is 1 1/2 times 100 (multiplicative thinking). The comparison between 100 and 150 can be stated many ways, all using ratio thinking, including the following:

  • The ratio of sixth graders to sevenths graders is the same as 150:100.
  • For every 15 sixth graders, there are 10 seventh graders.
  • For every 3 sixth graders, there are 2 seventh graders.
  • The ratio of sixth graders to seventh graders is 3:2.
  • For every 1 sixth grader, there are 2/3 seventh graders.
  • For every 1 seventh grader, there are 3/2 sixth graders.

The "for every" language is particularly useful because it leads to statements of equivalent ratios. The ratio 150:100 is equivalent to the ratio 3:2 (or 6:4 or 9:6 or 15:10 etc.) Equivalent ratios express the same comparison.
Part-to-Part, Part-to-Whole

Claim 1

The ratio of green cupcakes to the to the total number of cupcakes is 8 to 18.

Claim 4

1/3 of the cupcakes are green.

Claim 2

The ratio of green cupcakes to white cupcakes is 10 to 8.

Claim 5

The number of green cupcakes is half the number of white cupcakes.

Claim 3

The large box has 4 more green than the small box.

In the discussion of the preceding claims, students confirm the following:

  • Claim 1 compares the part of the box that is green cupcakes (8) to the total (whole) box (18).
  • Claim 2 intends to compare the part of the box that is green to the part that is white. However, the order of the ratio quantities is incorrect. That makes the ratio incorrect. The part-to-part statement should be: The ratio of green cupcakes to white cupcakes is 8 to 10.
  • Claim 3 compare the green part of one box (8) to the green part of the other box (4). BUT this is not stated as a ratio. It is stated as a difference.
  • Claim 4 can be restated as a ratio comparison of part to whole/total: In every 3 (total) cupcakes, there is 1 (part) green. The connection between fractions and ratios is complex.
  • Claim 5 can be restated as a part-to-part ratio: the ratio of green to white is 1:2, or for every 1/2 green, there is 1 white.

Rate, Unit Rate

A rate is a special kind of ratio, so all the ways of rephrasing or working with ratios also apply to rates. A rate is always a comparison of two different units, e.g miles per hour, dollars per gallon, etc. A unit rate is a rate where one of the quantities is 1. In the following example, 30 bars per $12 is equivalent to the. unit rte 2.5 bars per $1.

Students use tables to record and manipulate rates. By writing equivalent rates, they can fill out the table.

    30 bars: $12 or

    90 bars: $36 or

    150 bars: $60, etc

This table also makes it clear that there are two unit rates.

    2.5 bars: $1 or

    1 bar: $0.40

That is, there are two unit rates:

2.5 bars per $1 and 1 bar per $0.40.

Using Proportional Reasoning to Solve Problems

To reason proportionally, students might

  • write equivalent ratios, using the idea of scaling up or down a ratio;
  • make rate tables and use unit rates; or
  • use tape diagrams or double number lines, especially with percents.

Equivalent Ratios, Scaling

Slime recipe

  • 2 (4-ounce) bottles washable school glue
  • 1 teaspoon baking soda
  • 2 to 3 tablespoons saline solution (i.e. contact lens solution)

Suppose you make slime but you want to use 3 bottles of glue. How can you adjust the recipe so that the relationships between all quantities are the same?

Students may begin with the 2:3 representing the bottles of glue in the original recipe and the bottles of glue in the new recipe.

they can then scale up the recipe, making equivalent ratios for all the other ingredients, so that the comparison between original amounts and new amount stays 2:3 throughout. 2:3 = 1 tsp baking soda: 1 1/2 tsp baking soda etc.

The new amounts are 1 1/2 times the original.

Percents, Tape Diagrams, Double Number Lines

Suppose you want to know what percent 136 students is out of the total 400.

The underlying proportional reasoning is that

400 students: 100% = 136 student: ?%.

Tape diagrams, as shown here, are useful. Students might reason geometrically about what the marks represent on the scale: each mark is 1/100 of the total bar or 1% or 4 students.

Or they might reason numerically, 400:100 = 4:1 = 1: 1/4. Unit rtes are particularly helpful. Scaling up the unit rate 1:1/4 by multiplying by 136, we get 136:34.

These same tools can be used to answer questions like the following:

  • What quantity is 23% of 400?
  • What is the total if 104 is 26% of the total?

Converting Units Using Rate Tables

Suppose you have a walking rate of 3.6 miles per hour. What would that rate be if we converted the rate to miles per minute or minutes per mile or feet per mile? How far does a person walk in 8 minutes? (8 × 0.06 miles) How long does it take a person to walk 400 feet? (400 × 0.003 minutes)

Unit Arc of Learning

The development of reasoning with ratios continues throughout the rest of grade 6 at the analysis level. It deepens in several grade 7 units as proportional reasoning is used to reason within the context of rational numbers, similarity (scale drawings), probability, statistics, and linear relationships.

Correlation to Common Core State Standards for Mathematics

Click here to view the CCSSM correlations for Problems (including Mathematical Practice examples) and Assesments

Reasoning with Ratios. Understand ratios.

  • Understand ratios as a multiplicative comparison of two quantities and distinguish them from difference (additive) relationships
  • Understand that ratios can be either part-to-part or part-to-whole comparison
  • Understand unit rate as a ratio where one of the two quantities being compared has a value of 1
  • Understand that a percent is a part-to-whole ratio where the whole is 100
  • Recognize and represent equivalent ratios, including percents and unit rates, with ratio tables, tape diagrams, double number lines, and equations
  • Identify ratio and rate situations and choose appropriate representations and/or strategies to reason with ratios when solving problems
  • Use concepts associated with recognizing, representing, and using relationships between two variables, begun i the Variables and Patterns and Number Connections units, to work with ratio relationships

Mathematical Goals for the Unit

Note: Objectives in bold text are relevant to the current investigation.

Investigation 1: Making Comparisons

Students will be working to develop elements of the following Unit Goals throughout Investigation 1.

Reasoning with Ratios. Understand ratios.

  • Understand ratios as a multiplicative comparison of two quantities and distinguish them from difference (additive) relationships
  • Understand that ratios can be either part-to-part or part-to-whole comparison
  • Understand unit rate as a ratio where one of the two quantities being compared has a value of 1
  • Understand that a percent is a part-to-whole ratio where the whole is 100
  • Recognize and represent equivalent ratios, including percents and unit rates, with ratio tables, tape diagrams, double number lines, and equations
  • Identify ratio and rate situations and choose appropriate representations and/or strategies to reason with ratios when solving problems
  • Use concepts associated with recognizing, representing, and using relationships between two variables, begun i the Variables and Patterns and Number Connections units, to work with ratio relationships

Investigation 2: Using Ratios to Solve Problems

  • Understand unit rate as a ratio where one of the two quantities being compared has a value of 1
  • Understand that a percent is a part-to-whole ratio where the whole is 100
  • Recognize and represent equivalent ratios, including percents and unit rates, with ratio tables, tape diagrams, double number lines, and equations
  • Identify ratio and rate situations and choose appropriate representations and/or strategies to reason with ratios when solving problems
  • Use concepts associated with recognizing, representing, and using relationships between two variables, begun i the Variables and Patterns and Number Connections units, to work with ratio relationships

Students will be working to develop elements of the following Unit Goals throughout Investigation 3.

Reasoning with Ratios. Understand ratios.

  • Understand ratios as a multiplicative comparison of two quantities and distinguish them from difference (additive) relationships
  • Understand that ratios can be either part-to-part or part-to-whole comparison
  • Understand unit rate as a ratio where one of the two quantities being compared has a value of 1
  • Understand that a percent is a part-to-whole ratio where the whole is 100
  • Recognize and represent equivalent ratios, including percents and unit rates, with ratio tables, tape diagrams, double number lines, and equations

Investigation 4: For Every 100: Introducing Percents

Students will be working to develop elements of the following Unit Goals throughout Investigation 4.

Reasoning with Ratios. Understand ratios.

  • Understand ratios as a multiplicative comparison of two quantities and distinguish them from difference (additive) relationships
  • Understand that ratios can be either part-to-part or part-to-whole comparison
  • Understand unit rate as a ratio where one of the two quantities being compared has a value of 1
  • Understand that a percent is a part-to-whole ratio where the whole is 100
  • Recognize and represent equivalent ratios, including percents and unit rates, with ratio tables, tape diagrams, double number lines, and equations
  • Identify ratio and rate situations and choose appropriate representations and/or strategies to reason with ratios when solving problems
  • Use concepts associated with recognizing, representing, and using relationships between two variables, begun i the Variables and Patterns and Number Connections units, to work with ratio relationships
